Significant improvements in spatial accuracy and text-image alignment on DPG-Bench.
Demonstrated superior attribute binding, effectively preserving unique traits for multiple objects.
Validated the effectiveness of semantic priority grouping in reducing layout complexity.
Proved that explicit error-correction via a Checker agent significantly reduces rendering artifacts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce coDrawAgents, a multi-agent framework that uses collaborative reasoning to significantly improve compositional text-to-image accuracy.

title: Are Dependent Types in Set Theory Feasible?
summary: Following the types-as-sets paradigm, we present a mechanized embedding of dependent function types with a hierarchy of universes into schematic first-order logic with equality, with axiom schemas of Tarski-Grothendieck set theory. We carry this embedding in the Lisa proof assistant. On top of this foundation, we implement a proof-producing bidirectional type-checking tactic to compute proofs for typing judgements, with partial support for subtyping. We present examples showing how our approach enables automated reasoning for dependent types that is fully verified from set-theoretic axioms and deduction rules for schematic first-order logic with equality. Because types are merely sets, the resulting formalism supports equality that applies to all types and values and permits the usual substitution rules.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a mechanized **embedding of dependent function types** and a universe hierarchy into schematic first-order logic using **Tarski-Grothendieck (TG) set theory** axioms.
 • Implements a **proof-producing bidirectional type-checking tactic** within the Lisa proof assistant to automate the generation of formal proofs for typing judgements.
 • Leverages the **types-as-sets paradigm** to enable a unified formalism where set-theoretic equality and substitution rules apply universally to all types and values.
key_results: Successfully embedded dependent function types into schematic first-order logic with equality.
Integrated a hierarchy of universes compatible with Tarski-Grothendieck axioms.
Developed an automated bidirectional type-checking tactic for the Lisa proof assistant.
Demonstrated partial support for subtyping within a unified set-theoretic framework.
Enabled fully verified automated reasoning for dependent types starting from fundamental axioms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: EPFL researchers implement a mechanized embedding of dependent types in set theory to enable verified automated reasoning in Lisa.

title: Rethinking Multiple-Choice Questions for RLVR: Unlocking Potential via Distractor Design
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models. When applied to RLVR, Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs) offer a scalable source of verifiable data but risk inducing reward hacking, where models shortcut reasoning via random guessing or simple elimination. Current approaches often mitigate this by converting MCQs to open-ended formats, thereby discarding the contrastive signal provided by expert-designed distractors. In this work, we systematically investigate the impact of option design on RLVR. Our analysis highlights two primary insights: (1) Mismatches in option counts between training and testing degrade performance. (2) Strong distractors effectively mitigate random guessing, enabling effective RLVR training even with 2-way questions. Motivated by these findings, we propose Iterative Distractor Curation (IDC), a framework that actively constructs high-quality distractors to block elimination shortcuts and promote deep reasoning. Experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that our method effectively enhances distractor quality and yields significant gains in RLVR training compared to the original data.

processed.summary:
- Investigates **Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)** to improve LLM reasoning using Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs).
- Identifies **reward hacking** through random guessing and elimination shortcuts as a major barrier to effective RLVR training.
- Proposes **Iterative Distractor Curation (IDC)**, a framework that constructs high-quality distractors to force models into deeper reasoning paths.
- Demonstrates that **distractor quality** and matching option counts between train and test phases are critical for model stability.
key_results: Mismatches in the number of options between training and testing significantly degrade LLM reasoning performance.
Strong distractors effectively prevent models from using elimination shortcuts, enabling RLVR even on 2-way questions.
Converting MCQs to open-ended formats is counterproductive as it discards valuable contrastive signals from experts.
The IDC framework consistently produces higher-quality distractors than static, original datasets.
RLVR models trained with IDC distractors show significant gains in reasoning accuracy across multiple standard benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Iterative Distractor Curation to prevent reward hacking and enhance reasoning during RLVR training using MCQs.

title: Context is all you need: Towards autonomous model-based process design using agentic AI in flowsheet simulations
summary: Agentic AI systems integrating large language models (LLMs) with reasoning and tooluse capabilities are transforming various domains - in particular, software development. In contrast, their application in chemical process flowsheet modelling remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present an agentic AI framework that delivers assistance in an industrial flowsheet simulation environment. To this end, we show the capabilities of GitHub Copilot (GitHub, Inc., 2026), when using state-of-the-art LLMs, such as Claude Opus 4.6 (Anthropic, PBC, 2026), to generate valid syntax for our in-house process modelling tool Chemasim using the technical documentation and a few commented examples as context. Based on this, we develop a multi-agent system that decomposes process development tasks with one agent solving the abstract problem using engineering knowledge and another agent implementing the solution as Chemasim code. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework for typical flowsheet modelling examples, including (i) a reaction/separation process, (ii) a pressure-swing distillation, and (iii) a heteroazeotropic distillation including entrainer selection. Along these lines, we discuss current limitations of the framework and outline future research directions to further enhance its capabilities.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **multi-agent system (MAS)** architecture that decouples high-level **engineering reasoning** from low-level **code implementation** for chemical process modeling.
- Leverages **Claude Opus 4.6** within an agentic framework to generate valid syntax for **Chemasim**, a proprietary industrial flowsheet simulation tool, using technical documentation as context.
- Employs **In-context Learning (ICL)** and few-shot prompting to bridge the gap between general-purpose LLMs and niche, domain-specific programming languages.
- Evaluates the system's ability to perform **autonomous model-based design** for complex chemical tasks, reducing the manual effort required for simulation setup.
key_results: Successful generation of valid Chemasim syntax using documentation-based context.
Effective decomposition of tasks between a 'Reasoning Agent' and a 'Coding Agent'.
Automation of a complete reaction/separation process flowsheet.
Successful modeling of complex pressure-swing and heteroazeotropic distillation scenarios.
Demonstrated capability in autonomous entrainer selection for chemical separation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: BASF researchers introduce a multi-agent framework using Claude 4.6 to automate complex industrial chemical process flowsheet simulations.

title: Gaia GraL X.: The GraL catalogue of gravitationally lensed quasars Matched with \textit{Gaia} data, redshifts, and time delays
summary: Determining the Hubble constant tension requires alternative strategies, and multiply imaged quasars, with their intermediate redshifts, can potentially be used in this regard. We provide a currently complete catalogue of spectroscopically confirmed lensed quasars with ESA/{\it Gaia} astrometry and photometry, as well as redshifts and time delays when available. In addition to the improved astrometry, the catalogue increases the number of lensed quasars by a factor of 1.5 (now 364, of which 277 are doubles and 87 are quads or triples) and significantly increases the number of lensing galaxies detected (now 218), which represents a major step forward. Redshifts are provided for 347 quasars and 188 deflectors. A completely new table of time delays, required for estimates of $H_0$, is presented, with 195 time delays from 73 systems. {\it Gaia} absolute astrometry is sub-milliarcsecond and covers the entire sky. Future {\it Gaia} data releases will provide long-term photometry, which should provide many more time delays. The catalogues as presented here enable machine-learning techniques to be trained and tested and subsequently applied to the {\it Gaia} data releases. Finally, we derive simple but homogeneous models of the 18 quadruply imaged quasars for which images of all four components are presented in {\it Gaia} DR3.}

processed.summary:
- Release of the **GraL catalogue**, a comprehensive dataset of 364 spectroscopically confirmed **gravitationally lensed quasars** integrated with ESA/Gaia astrometry.
- The dataset provides a significant expansion of training data for **machine learning models** targeted at astronomical object classification and cosmological parameter estimation.
- Leverages sub-milliarcsecond **absolute astrometry** and long-term photometry to enable automated detection of time delays and lens modeling.
key_results: Increased the number of confirmed lensed quasars to 364, a 1.5x expansion.
Detected 218 lensing galaxies, facilitating detailed mass distribution studies.
Provided redshifts for 347 quasars and 188 deflector galaxies.
Compiled a new table of 195 time delays from 73 unique systems.
Derived homogeneous models for 18 quadruply imaged quasars using Gaia DR3 data.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The Gaia GraL collaboration releases an expanded lensed quasar dataset to facilitate machine learning applications in precision cosmology.

title: A Multi-task Large Reasoning Model for Molecular Science
summary: Advancements in artificial intelligence for molecular science are necessitating a paradigm shift from purely data-driven predictions to knowledge-guided computational reasoning. Existing molecular models are predominantly proprietary, lacking general molecular intelligence and generalizability. This underscores the necessity for computational methods that can effectively integrate scientific logic with deep learning architectures. Here we introduce a multi-task large reasoning model designed to emulate the cognitive processes of molecular scientists through structured reasoning and reflection. Our approach incorporates multi-specialist modules to provide versatile molecular expertise and a chain-of-thought (CoT) framework enhanced by reinforcement learning infused with molecular knowledge, enabling structured and reflective reasoning. Systematic evaluations across 10 molecular tasks and 47 metrics demonstrate that our model achieves an average 50.3% improvement over the base architecture, outperforming over 20 state-of-the-art baselines, including ultra-large-parameter foundation models, despite using significantly fewer training data and computational resources. This validates that embedding explicit reasoning mechanisms enables high-efficiency learning, allowing smaller-scale models to surpass massive counterparts in both efficacy and interpretability. The practical utility of this computational framework was validated through a case study on the design of central nervous system (CNS) drug candidates, illustrating its capacity to bridge data-driven and knowledge-integrated approaches for intelligent molecular design.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a **multi-task large reasoning model** that integrates **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** frameworks with **reinforcement learning** tailored for molecular logic.
- Employs **multi-specialist modules** to provide domain-specific expertise, enabling structured, reflective reasoning rather than simple pattern matching.
- Demonstrates extreme efficiency by outperforming **ultra-large-parameter foundation models** while using significantly less training data and computational resources.
- Validates the architecture via a **central nervous system (CNS)** drug design case study, bridging the gap between data-driven and knowledge-guided AI.
key_results: Achieved a 50.3% average performance improvement over the base model architecture.
Outperformed 20 state-of-the-art baselines, including massive proprietary foundation models.
Validated across 10 complex molecular tasks using 47 distinct evaluation metrics.
Demonstrated superior interpretability and efficiency compared to larger-scale counterparts.
Successfully identified potential CNS drug candidates through an integrated reasoning framework.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a knowledge-guided large reasoning model that outperforms massive foundation models in molecular design tasks.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Elliptical Cylinder Motion Control Tasks
summary: The control of devices with limited input always bring attention to solve by research due to its difficulty and non-trival solution. For instance, the inverted pendulum is benchmarking problem in control theory and machine learning. In this work, we are focused on the elliptical cylinder and its motion under limited torque. The inspiration of the problem is from untethered magnetic devices, which due to distance have to operate with limited input torque. In this work, the main goal is to define the control problem of elliptic cylinder with limited input torque and solve it by Reinforcement Learning. As a classical baseline, we evaluate a two-stage controller composed of an energy-shaping swing-up law and a local Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) stabilizer around the target equilibrium. The swing-up controller increases the system's mechanical energy to drive the state toward a neighborhood of the desired equilibrium, a linearization of the nonlinear model yields an LQR that regulates the angle and angular-rate states to the target orientation with bounded input. This swing-up + LQR policy is a strong, interpretable reference for underactuated system and serves a point of comparison to the learned policy under identical limits and parameters. The solution shows that the learning is possible however, the different cases like stabilization in upward position or rotating of half turn are very difficult for increasing mass or ellipses with a strongly unequal perimeter ratio.

processed.summary:
* Evaluates **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** for controlling elliptical cylinder motion under limited torque, simulating constraints found in **untethered magnetic devices**.
* Implements a benchmarking framework comparing RL against a **two-stage controller** consisting of energy-shaping swing-up laws and a **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)**.
* Focuses on the transition from nonlinear swing-up dynamics to local stabilization at target orientations using **underactuated system** principles.
* Analyzes the impact of physical parameters, such as **mass distribution** and perimeter ratios, on the stability and convergence of learned policies.
key_results: RL agents successfully learn to manage elliptical cylinder motion despite significant torque limitations.
The hybrid Swing-up + LQR policy provides a highly interpretable and strong baseline for performance comparison.
Upward stabilization tasks remain difficult for RL agents as the mass of the cylinder increases.
Rotating the cylinder by a half-turn is particularly challenging when dealing with unequal perimeter ratios.
The study confirms that while RL is a viable solution for nonlinear control, physical geometry heavily influences learning efficiency.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that Reinforcement Learning can control underactuated elliptical cylinders, though performance scales poorly with mass.

title: GLEAM: A Multimodal Imaging Dataset and HAMM for Glaucoma Classification
summary: We propose glaucoma lesion evaluation and analysis with multimodal imaging (GLEAM), the first publicly available tri-modal glaucoma dataset comprising scanning laser ophthalmoscopy fundus images, circumpapillary OCT images, and visual field pattern deviation maps, annotated with four disease stages, enabling effective exploitation of multimodal complementary information and facilitating accurate diagnosis and treatment across disease stages. To effectively integrate cross-modal information, we propose hierarchical attentive masked modeling (HAMM) for multimodal glaucoma classification. Our framework employs hierarchical attentive encoders and light decoders to focus cross-modal representation learning on the encoder.

processed.summary:
• Introduction of **GLEAM**, the first public **tri-modal dataset** for glaucoma, integrating SLO fundus, OCT images, and Visual Field (VF) maps.
• Proposes **Hierarchical Attentive Masked Modeling (HAMM)** to fuse structural and functional imaging data through specialized cross-modal integration.
• Employs **hierarchical attentive encoders** and light decoders to concentrate representation learning on the encoder side.
• Facilitates granular **multimodal classification** across four distinct disease stages by leveraging complementary cross-modal information.
key_results: Released the first tri-modal public dataset (GLEAM) for glaucoma research.
Integrated SLO fundus, circumpapillary OCT, and VF pattern deviation maps.
Developed the HAMM framework for effective cross-modal feature representation.
Achieved accurate glaucoma diagnosis across four distinct disease stages.
Validated the efficiency of hierarchical attention in masked modeling architectures.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce GLEAM and HAMM, a tri-modal dataset and hierarchical masking framework for improved multimodal glaucoma classification.

title: Think and Answer ME: Benchmarking and Exploring Multi-Entity Reasoning Grounding in Remote Sensing
summary: Recent advances in reasoning language models and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards have significantly enhanced multi-step reasoning capabilities. This progress motivates the extension of reasoning paradigms to remote sensing visual grounding task. However, existing remote sensing grounding methods remain largely confined to perception-level matching and single-entity formulations, limiting the role of explicit reasoning and inter-entity modeling. To address this challenge, we introduce a new benchmark dataset for Multi-Entity Reasoning Grounding in Remote Sensing (ME-RSRG). Based on ME-RSRG, we reformulate remote sensing grounding as a multi-entity reasoning task and propose an Entity-Aware Reasoning (EAR) framework built upon visual-linguistic foundation models. EAR generates structured reasoning traces and subject-object grounding outputs. It adopts supervised fine-tuning for cold-start initialization and is further optimized via entity-aware reward-driven Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Extensive experiments on ME-RSRG demonstrate the challenges of multi-entity reasoning and verify the effectiveness of our proposed EAR framework. Our dataset, code, and models will be available at https://github.com/CV-ShuchangLyu/ME-RSRG.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **ME-RSRG** dataset, shifting remote sensing visual grounding from simple perception to **multi-entity reasoning** involving complex inter-entity relationships.
- Proposes the **Entity-Aware Reasoning (EAR)** framework, which leverages **visual-linguistic foundation models** to generate structured reasoning traces and subject-object grounding outputs.
- Implements a two-stage training approach using **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** for cold-start initialization followed by **Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)**.
- Utilizes **verifiable rewards** for entity-aware reinforcement learning, significantly enhancing the model's ability to ground multiple entities through explicit reasoning.
key_results: Established a new benchmark (ME-RSRG) specifically for multi-entity spatial reasoning in remote sensing.
Proved that explicit reasoning traces improve grounding accuracy compared to direct perception-matching models.
Validated that GRPO-based RL optimization outperforms standard SFT in handling complex entity relationships.
Demonstrated successful subject-object grounding in high-resolution aerial imagery.
Provided an open-source framework (EAR) and dataset to bridge the gap between reasoning models and remote sensing.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Shuchang Lyu et al. introduce EAR, leveraging GRPO and structured reasoning to improve multi-entity grounding in remote sensing.

title: Exploring the role of embodiment on intimacy perception in a multiparty collaborative task
summary: During collaborative board games, cohesion represents a key aspect to define a well functionning group. From the success of the task to the developement of interpersonal relationship, this concept covers many aspects of group dynamics. The goal of our work is to investigate the factors that impact cohesion in a group, and specifically the relevant social skills that improve collaboration between multiple entities. In this article, we focus on the role of embodiement on different aspects of an interaction. We propose an experimental protocol, based on a collected corpus of humans playing a collaborative board game, to study how different agents' embodiment affect the perception of these agents and of the group as a whole. We conclude by presenting an outline of the problematics of the conception of the protocol and of multi-agent system related challenges.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **experimental protocol** to analyze the effects of **agent embodiment** on group cohesion and intimacy during multiparty collaborative tasks.
- Utilizes a collected **human-interaction corpus** of board game play to model and benchmark the perception of synthetic entities within a group.
- Investigates the **social skills** necessary for improving collaboration between humans and agents in **Multi-Agent Systems (MAS)**.
- Addresses the architectural challenges of designing **socially-aware agents** that can foster interpersonal relationships and functional group dynamics.
key_results: Definition of a structured protocol for evaluating embodiment in social interactions.
Analysis of social factors that directly impact group cohesion during tasks.
Establishment of intimacy perception as a metric for human-agent collaboration.
Identification of specific multi-agent system challenges in collaborative board game settings.
Validation of using human-to-human interaction corpora for synthetic agent behavior modeling.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose an experimental protocol to evaluate how agent embodiment affects group cohesion and intimacy in collaborative tasks.

title: The RIGID Framework: Research-Integrated, Generative AI-Mediated Instructional Design
summary: Instructional Design (ID) often faces challenges in incorporating research-based knowledge and pedagogical best practices. Although educational researchers and government agencies emphasize grounding ID in evidence, integrating research findings into everyday design workflows is often complex, as it requires considering multiple context-specific demands and constraints. To address this persistent gap, this paper explores how research in the learning sciences (LS) can be systematically integrated across ID workflows and how recent advances in generative AI can help operationalize this integration. While ID and LS share a commitment to improving learning experiences through design-oriented approaches in authentic contexts, structured integration between the two fields remains limited, leaving their complementary insights underutilized. We present RIGID (Research-Integrated, Generative AI-Mediated Instructional Design), a unified framework that integrates LS research across ID workflows spanning analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation phases, while leveraging generative AI to mediate this integration at each stage. The RIGID framework provides a systematic approach for enabling research-integrated instructional design that is both operational and context-sensitive, while preserving the central role of human expertise.

processed.summary:
* The **RIGID framework** establishes a structured methodology for embedding **Learning Sciences (LS)** research directly into the **Instructional Design (ID)** lifecycle.
* It leverages **Generative AI** as a mediation layer to operationalize complex, evidence-based pedagogical theories into actionable design outputs during analysis and implementation.
* The architecture emphasizes a **human-in-the-loop** approach, ensuring AI-generated insights remain context-sensitive and subject to expert verification.
key_results: Bridging the gap between theoretical pedagogical research and practical design workflows.
Systematic integration of AI across analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation phases.
Reduced cognitive load for designers attempting to apply multi-contextual research findings.
Operationalization of context-sensitive, research-grounded instructional materials.
Maintenance of human expertise as the central governing factor in AI-mediated design.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the RIGID framework to integrate learning science research into instructional design workflows using Generative AI.

title: Hydrogen-atom roaming reactions in water clusters: Unveiling an unusual dimension of water reactivity through first-principles calculations and machine learning
summary: Water mediates a broad range of chemical reactions, including proton transfer, bond rearrangement, and conventional radical processes, defining a continuously expanding repertoire of intrinsic reactivity. However, roaming, a fundamental reaction mechanism that a departing fragment bypasses the minimum energy path to recombine, has not been identified in water itself. Here, we report the discovery of hydrogen-atom roaming reactions in water clusters through high-precision ab initio calculations of first-principles. A neutral hydrogen atom departs as a radical, roams across the flat potential energy surface, and recombines along pathways that connect the same reactants and products as known hydrogen-bond network rearrangements. Interpretable machine learning analysis identifies the reactant dipole moment as the decisive switch governing whether roaming occurs, underpinned by exchange-repulsion and electrostatic interactions. Once roaming is initiated, polarizability and spin population determine barrier heights, while the charge distribution of the roaming hydrogen atom governs barrier widths, collectively shaped by electrostatic, orbital, and dispersion contributions. These findings establish hydrogen-atom roaming as a previously unrecognized intrinsic reaction class in water, complementing a fundamental dimension to the mechanistic picture of water reactivity.

processed.summary:
* Leverages **high-precision ab initio calculations** combined with **interpretable machine learning** to identify a novel hydrogen-atom roaming mechanism in water clusters.
* Utilizes ML to pinpoint the **reactant dipole moment** as the critical decision-making feature that triggers roaming behavior over traditional pathways.
* Characterizes the potential energy surface by correlating **polarizability** and **spin population** with barrier kinetics using statistical modeling.
* Provides a new **mechanistic framework** for water reactivity that integrates radical processes into the existing understanding of hydrogen-bond rearrangements.
key_results: Identification of hydrogen-atom roaming as a previously unknown intrinsic reaction class in water clusters.
Discovery that the reactant dipole moment acts as the decisive 'switch' for initiating roaming reactions.
ML analysis revealed that barrier heights are fundamentally determined by polarizability and spin population.
Barrier widths were found to be governed primarily by the charge distribution of the roaming hydrogen atom.
Successful integration of electrostatic, exchange-repulsion, and dispersion contributions into a unified reactivity model.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers use interpretable machine learning to discover hydrogen-atom roaming reactions, adding a new dimension to our understanding of water.

title: SectEval: Evaluating the Latent Sectarian Preferences of Large Language Models
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) becomes a popular source for religious knowledge, it is important to know if it treats different groups fairly. This study is the first to measure how LLMs handle the differences between the two main sects of Islam: Sunni and Shia. We present a test called SectEval, available in both English and Hindi, consisting of 88 questions, to check the bias-ness of 15 top LLM models, both proprietary and open-weights. Our results show a major inconsistency based on language. In English, many powerful models DeepSeek-v3 and GPT-4o often favored Shia answers. However, when asked the exact same questions in Hindi, these models switched to favoring Sunni answers. This means a user could get completely different religious advice just by changing languages. We also looked at how models react to location. Advanced models Claude-3.5 changed their answers to match the user's country-giving Shia answers to a user from Iran and Sunni answers to a user from Saudi Arabia. In contrast, smaller models (especially in Hindi) ignored the user's location and stuck to a Sunni viewpoint. These findings show that AI is not neutral; its religious ``truth'' changes depending on the language you speak and the country you claim to be from. The data set is available at https://github.com/secteval/SectEval/

processed.summary:
- Introduces **SectEval**, a specialized benchmark designed to evaluate **latent sectarian preferences** within Islamic religious contexts for both proprietary and open-weight LLMs.
- Evaluates **cross-lingual consistency** across English and Hindi, revealing that models frequently flip sectarian alignment based solely on the input language.
- Investigates **contextual grounding**, demonstrating that advanced reasoning models like Claude-3.5 dynamically adjust religious outputs to align with a user's **geographical location**.
- Highlights significant **alignment fragility** in smaller models, which often ignore user context in favor of a default majority viewpoint.
key_results: SectEval consists of 88 targeted religious questions available in English and Hindi to test Sunni/Shia bias.
GPT-4o and DeepSeek-v3 favored Shia viewpoints in English but switched to Sunni viewpoints when prompted in Hindi.
Claude-3.5 exhibited high sensitivity to location, providing Shia-aligned answers for Iran and Sunni-aligned for Saudi Arabia.
Smaller models frequently defaulted to a Sunni viewpoint in Hindi regardless of the user's stated geographical context.
The study proves that LLM neutrality is a 'myth' that varies significantly by language and geopolitical metadata.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The SectEval researchers demonstrate that LLM religious preferences flip based on language and location, revealing significant latent sectarian biases.

title: TerraFlow: Multimodal, Multitemporal Representation Learning for Earth Observation
summary: We propose TerraFlow, a novel approach to multimodal, multitemporal learning for Earth observation. TerraFlow builds on temporal training objectives that enable sequence-aware learning across space, time, and modality, while remaining robust to the variable-length inputs commonly encountered in real-world Earth observation data. Our experiments demonstrate superiority of TerraFlow over state-of-the-art foundation models for Earth observation across all temporal tasks of the GEO-Bench-2 benchmark. We additionally demonstrate that TerraFlow is able to make initial steps towards deep-learning based risk map prediction for natural disasters -- a task on which other state-of-the-art foundation models frequently collapse. TerraFlow outperforms state-of-the-art foundation models by up to 50% in F1 score and 24% in Brier score.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **TerraFlow**, a novel foundation model designed for **multimodal and multitemporal** representation learning in Earth Observation.
- Implements **temporal training objectives** that enable sequence-aware learning across space and time, specifically addressing the challenge of **variable-length inputs**.
- Demonstrates unique capabilities in **natural disaster risk map prediction**, a complex task where previous state-of-the-art models typically fail or collapse.
- Achieves superior performance across all temporal tasks within the **GEO-Bench-2** benchmark, significantly advancing the state of generative AI for geospatial data.
key_results: Outperformed state-of-the-art foundation models on all GEO-Bench-2 temporal tasks.
Achieved up to a 50% improvement in F1 score compared to existing models.
Delivered a 24% improvement in Brier score for more accurate probabilistic modeling.
Successfully maintained robustness across varying temporal sequence lengths in real-world data.
Enabled deep-learning based risk map prediction for disasters where other models collapsed.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce TerraFlow, a multitemporal foundation model that improves Earth observation F1 scores by up to 50%.

title: HIFICL: High-Fidelity In-Context Learning for Multimodal Tasks
summary: In-Context Learning (ICL) is a significant paradigm for Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), using a few in-context demonstrations (ICDs) for new task adaptation. However, its performance is sensitive to demonstration configurations and computationally expensive. Mathematically, the influence of these demonstrations can be decomposed into a dynamic mixture of the standard attention output and the context values. Current approximation methods simplify this process by learning a "shift vector". Inspired by the exact decomposition, we introduce High-Fidelity In-Context Learning (HIFICL) to more faithfully model the ICL mechanism. HIFICL consists of three key components: 1) a set of "virtual key-value pairs" to act as a learnable context, 2) a low-rank factorization for stable and regularized training, and 3) a simple end-to-end training objective. From another perspective, this mechanism constitutes a form of context-aware Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT). Extensive experiments show that HiFICL consistently outperforms existing approximation methods on several multimodal benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/bbbandari/HiFICL.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **HIFICL**, a high-fidelity framework for **In-Context Learning (ICL)** that replaces sensitive, expensive demonstrations with **virtual key-value pairs** as a learnable context.
* Utilizes a **low-rank factorization** architecture to ensure stable training and regularization, effectively framing ICL as a **context-aware Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)** method.
* Improves upon traditional approximation methods by faithfully modeling the mathematical decomposition of **attention outputs** and context values in **Large Multimodal Models (LMMs)**.
key_results: Consistently outperforms existing 'shift vector' approximation methods in multimodal tasks.
Provides a more computationally efficient alternative to processing long sequences of in-context demonstrations.
Demonstrates superior performance across several competitive multimodal benchmarks.
Establishes a stable end-to-end training objective for integrating learned context into attention mechanisms.
Publicly releases code to facilitate adoption of high-fidelity context modeling in LMM workflows.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HiFICL researchers introduce a low-rank virtual key-value mechanism to improve multimodal in-context learning through parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
